title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_71 × Z_25) prime?
generalization_signal: At p = 71 the fixed A0 reservoir and the high-barrier zero-spill front still survive, but the prime-only complementary pool for C is short by exactly one. The exact feeder appears to close after adding one complementary-support label 25, so the live family question shifts from a prime-only C theorem to a one-supplement complementary-support slice.
strongest_honest_claim: An explicit prime-labeling witness for Γ(Z_71 × Z_25) survives skeptical verification with A fixed to A0 = {2,3,4,6,8,9,12,16,18,24,27,32,36,48,54,64,72,81,96,108}, B = {1,191,193,197}, C equal to the 69 primes in {1,...,374} outside {2,3,191,193,197} together with 25, and D the residual complement. This is still not a proved family theorem, but it is strong feeder evidence that the strict prime-only complementary-pool slice fails first at p = 71 while a one-supplement complementary-support repair still works.
candidate_theorem_slice: A one-supplement high-barrier / complementary-support slice for Γ(Z_p × Z_25): with A fixed to A0 and B = {1,b1,b2,b3} chosen from barrier primes above half the interval, if the complementary-support pool against {2,3,b1,b2,b3} contains p - 1 labels with at most one non-prime supplement, then the four-class reduction yields a prime labeling.
next_action: generalize the verified p = 71 feeder as the first prime-only failure / one-supplement repair on the q = 5 line, then decide whether that refined slice warrants Lean support lemmas before any exact-instance Lean work
